metadata.summary: Cache embedding vectors to avoid redundant API calls: in-memory LRU, file-based JSON, and Redis (production) patterns with model-namespaced cache keys.

content.title: Embedding Caching Patterns
content.summary: Embedding the same text twice wastes API cost and latency. Cache embeddings by a model-namespaced hash of the text. In production, use Redis with a 30-day TTL. Typical RAG workloads see 70-90% cache hit rates on repeat queries and re-ingested documents.

why.p: Cache hit rate of 80% produces 80% cost reduction on embedding API spend. Repeat queries in RAG systems are common — users ask similar questions, documents are re-ingested during incremental updates. Redis cache keys must include the model name — reusing the same key format for multiple models causes silent cross-model cache collisions where a vector from model A is returned for a request from model B.
Cache invalidation: text-based cache keys must include the model name — different models produce different vectors for the same text. Cache hit rate of 80% = 80% cost reduction on embedding spend.

list.item: High-volume embedding workloads where documents or queries repeat across requests.
RAG systems with incremental ingestion — new documents added to a mostly-stable corpus.
Production systems where embedding API cost is measurable.
Any workload calling the same embedding model for overlapping text sets.
One-time batch ingestion of a fully unique corpus — cache miss rate will be 100% and cache adds overhead.
Streaming real-time embeddings where latency matters more than cost — Redis round-trip may exceed the embedding API latency for small models.

section.name: in-memory-cache
section.p: Use Python's lru_cache for small workloads where persistence is not required. Limited to process lifetime and maxsize entries.

example.title: LRU in-memory embedding cache
example.note: lru_cache requires hashable arguments. Tuples are hashable; lists are not — convert the returned list to tuple for storage.

section.name: file-based-cache
section.p: File-based JSON cache persists across process restarts. Suitable for local development and infrequent batch jobs. Not suitable for concurrent multi-process access.

example.title: File-based embedding cache
example.note: Cache key includes model name to prevent cross-model collisions. Each vector stored as a separate JSON file — avoids holding the entire cache in memory.

section.name: redis-cache
section.p: Redis is the production caching backend for embeddings. Supports batch retrieval via pipeline, TTL-based expiry, and concurrent multi-process access.

rule.statement: Redis cache keys MUST be namespaced as emb:{model}:{sha256-of-model-and-text}. Cache TTL MUST be set (30 days is standard). Batch retrieval MUST use Redis pipeline to avoid N round-trips.
rule.rationale: Without model namespace in the key, a cache populated by text-embedding-3-small will silently return wrong vectors for text-embedding-3-large requests — the text is identical but the vector spaces differ. TTL prevents unbounded memory growth. Pipeline reduces N Redis round-trips to 1.

example.title: Redis embedding cache with pipeline batch retrieval
example.note: Key format emb:{model}:{hash} namespaces by model. Pipeline collapses N GET calls into 1 round-trip — essential for batch cache lookups of 50-100 texts.

section.name: batch-cached-embed
section.p: Full production pattern: batch embed with Redis cache check, token-limit guard, and fallback to API for uncached texts.

example.title: Batch embed with Redis cache and token guard
example.code:
import hashlib, json, tiktoken import redis as redis_lib
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base") rc = redis_lib.from_url("redis://localhost:6379")
def embed_batch_cached(client, texts: list[str], model: str, max_tokens: int = 8000) -> list[list[float]]: results = [None] * len(texts) to_embed = [] # (original_index, text, cache_key)
for i, text in enumerate(texts): token_count = len(enc.encode(text)) if token_count > max_tokens: text = enc.decode(enc.encode(text)[:max_tokens]) # truncate with warning
key = f"emb:{model}:{hashlib.sha256((model + text).encode()).hexdigest()}" cached = rc.get(key) if cached: results[i] = json.loads(cached) else: to_embed.append((i, text, key))
if to_embed: resp = client.embeddings.create(input=[t for _, t, _ in to_embed], model=model) for j, (i, _, key) in enumerate(to_embed): emb = resp.data[j].embedding rc.setex(key, 86400 * 30, json.dumps(emb)) results[i] = emb
return results
example.note: Returns results in the same order as input texts. Cache hits bypass the API call entirely — only uncached texts go to the embedding API in one batch.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A separate caching layer (Redis) intercepts embed requests and returns cached vectors for previously seen texts. The ingestion agent reports: embedded count, cache hits, skipped (over token limit), and total tokens.
section.prompt: You are an embedding ingestion agent. Given: texts[], model_name, batch_size=100. For each batch: 1. Check token count per text — skip any exceeding 8000 tokens (log warning). 2. Check Redis cache — retrieve cached embeddings for texts already seen. 3. For uncached texts: call embed API in one batch request. 4. Store results to Redis cache (TTL=30 days). 5. Return: {"embedded": N, "cache_hits": M, "skipped": K, "total_tokens": T}

antipattern.description: Cache keys that include only text hash without model name: emb:{sha256-of-text}.
antipattern.reason: When a pipeline switches models (e.g., text-embedding-3-small to text-embedding-3-large), existing cache entries for the old model are returned for the new model's requests. The vectors are from different spaces — retrieval produces wrong results silently.

antipattern.description: No TTL on Redis cache keys.
antipattern.reason: Embedding vectors for deleted or updated documents accumulate indefinitely. Redis memory grows unbounded. Cache key space becomes stale over time.
